# Synthetic biology 2020–2030: six commercially-available products that are changing our world

[*Nature Communications*](/ncomms)
**volume 11**, Article number: 6379 (2020)
[Cite this article](#citeas)

152k Accesses

685 Altmetric

[Metrics details](/articles/s41467-020-20122-2/metrics)

### Subjects

Synthetic biology will transform how we grow food, what we eat, and where we source materials and medicines. Here I have selected six products that are now on the market, highlighting the underlying technologies and projecting forward to the future that can be expected over the next ten years.

“The time has come for synthetic biologists to develop more real-world applications […] the field has had its hype phase, now it needs to deliver.” So concluded an infamous article in 2010[1](/articles/s41467-020-20122-2#ref-CR1 "Kwok, R. Five hard truths for synthetic biology. Nature 463, 288–290 (2010)."). Early research struggled to design cells and physically build DNA with pre-2010 projects often failing due to uncertainty and variability. Since then, rapid technological advances occurred that are well-reviewed in this series of commentaries[2](/articles/s41467-020-20122-2#ref-CR2 "Meng, F. & Ellis, T. The second decade of synthetic biology: 2010-2020. Nat. Commun. 11, 5174 (2020)."). Products from synthetic biology are rapidly permeating society and by 2030, it is highly likely that you will have eaten, worn, used or been treated with one.

While there are many biotechnology, pharmaceutical and agriculture companies, I selected those products that best highlight the application of synthetic biology tools developed 2000–2020 and are available now or by early 2021[2](#ref-CR2 "Meng, F. & Ellis, T. The second decade of synthetic biology: 2010-2020. Nat. Commun. 11, 5174 (2020)."),[3](#ref-CR3 "Renewable Chemical Platforms Building the Biobased Economy. BIO Chemical Companies Report (Biotechnology Innovation Organization, Washington, D.C., 2018)."),[4](/articles/s41467-020-20122-2#ref-CR4 "Cumbers, J., Murray, J., Costa, K. & Schmidt, C. Synthetic Biology Startups Raised $3 Billion In The First Half Of 2020 (Synbiobeta, 2020)."). The first three represent chemicals produced by engineered cells or enzymes (leghemoglobin, sitgaliptin, diamines) that are isolated and purified (Fig. [1](/articles/s41467-020-20122-2#Fig1)). For the second three, the products are the engineered cells themselves (engineered bacteria, CAR-Ts, genome edited soy). The development of these was enabled by advances in metabolic engineering, directed evolution (awarded the 2018 Nobel Prize), automated strain engineering, metagenomic discovery, gene circuit design, and genome editing (awarded the 2020 Nobel Prize)[5](/articles/s41467-020-20122-2#ref-CR5 "Doudna, J. & Charpentier, E. The new fronteir of genome engineering with CRISPR-Cas9. Science 346, 6213 (2014)."),[6](/articles/s41467-020-20122-2#ref-CR6 "Arnold, F. H. Innovation by Evolution: Bringing New Chemistry to Life. Nobel Lecture 
                  https://www.nobelprize.org/prizes/chemistry/2018/arnold/lecture/
                  
                 (2018).").

![figure 1](//media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs41467-020-20122-2/MediaObjects/41467_2020_20122_Fig1_HTML.png)

(Top to bottom) Heme being produced to make an Impossible Burger. Sitgaliptin, with the enzymatically produced stereocenter shown in red. Hyaline transparent and flexible film. ProveN fertilizer being applied to corn. Kymriah, produced by genetically modifying a patient’s T cells. Calyno high-oleic oil from soy. Images were reproduced with permission from Impossible Foods, Zymergen, Pivot Bio, Novartis, and Calyxt, respectively.

## Products from cells

### “Burgers that bleed” by impossible foods

Impossible Foods recognized that blood, specifically the iron-containing heme, is important for the taste and experience of eating a hamburger[7](/articles/s41467-020-20122-2#ref-CR7 "Varadan, R. et al. Ground Meat Replicas (Impossible Foods Inc., 2019)."). Some plant roots “bleed” when cut. The yeast *Pichia pastoris* was engineered to produce soy leghemoglobin, which improves meaty flavors and aromas when added to a plant-based burger[8](/articles/s41467-020-20122-2#ref-CR8 "Shankar, S. & Hoyt, M. A. Expression Constructs and Methods of Genetically Engineering Methylotrophic Yeast, (ed USPTO) (Impossible Foods, USA, 2020)."). Optimizing the production strain involved DNA synthesis, Gibson Assembly, genetic part libraries and a positive feedback loop for autoinduction. Compared to a beef patty, the Impossible Burger requires 96% less land and 89% fewer greenhouse gases. Worldwide, their products are available in over 30,000 restaurants and 15,000 grocery stores.

Advances in metabolic engineering have simplified the transfer of a pathway from its natural source to a production host, making it possible to move larger pathways to yeast with less effort[9](/articles/s41467-020-20122-2#ref-CR9 "Cravens, A., Payne, J. & Smolke, C. D. Synthetic biology strategies for microbial biosynthesis of plant natural products. Nat. Commun. 10, 2142 (2019)."). This includes computational tools for pathway design, methods to discover and modify enzymes so that they can be ported from plants, precision part libraries to balance their expression, techniques to assemble and integrate large multi-gene pathways and genome editing to redirect carbon flux[9](#ref-CR9 "Cravens, A., Payne, J. & Smolke, C. D. Synthetic biology strategies for microbial biosynthesis of plant natural products. Nat. Commun. 10, 2142 (2019)."),[10](#ref-CR10 "Lee, M. E., DeLoache, W. C., Cerbantes, B. & Dueber, J. E. A highly characterized yeast toolkit for modular, multipart assembly. ACS Synth. Biol. 4, 975 (2015)."),[11](/articles/s41467-020-20122-2#ref-CR11 "Heirendt, L. et al. Creation and analysis of biochemical contraint-based models using the COBRA Toolbox v.3.0. Nat. Protoc. 14, 639 (2019)."). The palate of food additives that are obtained from engineered yeast is growing rapidly, with products emerging that contain Vitamin E (DSM), Stevia (Amyris and DSM) and milk whey (Perfect Day). Similarly, plant pathways to pharmaceuticals have been moved into yeast, including taxadiene (Taxol precursor), steroids, THC and opiates and efforts are underway to scale-up these processes[9](/articles/s41467-020-20122-2#ref-CR9 "Cravens, A., Payne, J. & Smolke, C. D. Synthetic biology strategies for microbial biosynthesis of plant natural products. Nat. Commun. 10, 2142 (2019)."),[12](/articles/s41467-020-20122-2#ref-CR12 "Lin, G.-M., Warden-Rothman, R. & Voigt, C. A. Retrosynthetic design of metabolic pathways to chemicals not found in nature. Curr. Opin. Sys. Biol. 14, 82 (2019)."),[13](/articles/s41467-020-20122-2#ref-CR13 "Xu, S. & Li, Y. Yeast as a promising heterologous host for steroid bioproduction. J. Ind. Microbiol. Biotechnol. s10295–020-02291-7 (2020)."). An early landmark achievement to make the anti-malarial Artimisin was taken into production by Sanofi, but was discontinued because the cost was higher than sourcing it from the plant[14](/articles/s41467-020-20122-2#ref-CR14 "Ro, D.-K. et al. Production of the antimalarial drug precursor artemisinic acid in engineered yeast. Nature 440, 940 (2006)."),[15](/articles/s41467-020-20122-2#ref-CR15 "Peplow, M. Looking for Cheaper Routes to Making Medicines. (C&E News, 2018)."). Animal pathways can also be reconstructed; for example, producing shark squalene for vaccines and moth hormones to disrupt an agricultural pest[16](/articles/s41467-020-20122-2#ref-CR16 "Chen, X., Gentili, M., Hacohen, N. & Regev, A. A cell-free antibody engineering platform rapidly generates SARS-CoV-2 neutralizing antibodies. Preprint at 
                  https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.1101/2020.10.29.361287v1
                  
                 (2020)."),[17](/articles/s41467-020-20122-2#ref-CR17 "Holkenbrink, C. et al. Production of moth sex phermones for pest control by yeast fermentation. Metab. Eng. 62, 312 (2020)."). Bioinformatics and high-throughput DNA construction can be used to transfer pathways *en mass* from a plant or microbial community into a production host, for example to screen the human microbiome for putative pharmaceuticals[18](/articles/s41467-020-20122-2#ref-CR18 "Milshteyn, A., Colosimo, D. A. & Brady, S. F. Accessing bioactive natural products from the human microbiome. Cell Host Microbe 23, 725 (2018)."),[19](/articles/s41467-020-20122-2#ref-CR19 "Kautsar, S. A., Suarez Duran, H. G., Blin, K., Osbourn, A. & Medema, M. H. plantiSMASH: automated identification, annotation and expression analysis of plant biosynthetic gene clusters. Nucleic Acids Res. 45, W55 (2017)."). Transferring pathways to fermentation-friendly chasses facilitate access to chemicals present in low quantities in nature and serves as a platform for their enzymatic diversification to produce new molecules.

### Januvia, a diabetes drug from Merck

Januvia (sitgaliptin) increases insulin secretion by inhibiting dipeptidyl peptidase 4. It is the 95th most-prescribed drug with ~107 prescriptions and $1.35 billion in annual sales[20](/articles/s41467-020-20122-2#ref-CR20 "Kane, S. The Top 300 of 2020 (2020)."),[21](/articles/s41467-020-20122-2#ref-CR21 "Johnson, L. A. Merck 1Q net quadruples as cancer drug, vaccine sales soar, Medical Press (2019)."). Stigaliptin has one stereospecific amine that is challenging to manufacture using chemistry alone, requiring heavy metals and high pressures[22](/articles/s41467-020-20122-2#ref-CR22 "Savile, C. K. et al. Biocatalytic asymmetric synthesis of chiral amines from ketones applied to sitagliptin manufacture. Science 329, 305 (2010)."). Starting with a (R)-selective transaminase from *Arthrobacter* sp., the computational design was applied to “open up” the binding pocket to new substrates, followed by rounds directed evolution[6](/articles/s41467-020-20122-2#ref-CR6 "Arnold, F. H. Innovation by Evolution: Bringing New Chemistry to Life. Nobel Lecture 
                  https://www.nobelprize.org/prizes/chemistry/2018/arnold/lecture/
                  
                 (2018).") to improve its activity under manufacturing conditions. The final enzyme has 27 amino acid substitutions and can achieve >99.95% enantiomeric excess. This approach has also been applied to manufacture the HIV antiviral islatravir for Phase 2 clinical trials using a cascade of five enzymes, all of which are the products of directed evolution[23](/articles/s41467-020-20122-2#ref-CR23 "Huffman, M. A. et al. Design of an in vitro biocatalytic cascade for the manufacture of islatravir. Science 366, 1255 (2019)."). The starting compounds for islatravir and sitgaliptin are highly fluorinated or have an alkyne group that would be difficult to produce using enzymes, requiring chemical steps in manufacturing.

Molecules made by biology can be the envy of chemists, with long retrosynthetic routes to their creation populating the tomes of synthetic organic chemistry[24](/articles/s41467-020-20122-2#ref-CR24 "Nicolaou, K. C. & Sorensen, E. J. Classics in Total Synthesis. (Wiley-VCH, New York, 1996)."). One is tempted to declare biology the ultimate chemist and point to a future where all chemicals are produced by enzymes in cells. This is, however, not accurate. All of the chemistry performed by the natural world can be captured with ~250 reactions, whereas there are 60,000+ in the chemistry literature[12](/articles/s41467-020-20122-2#ref-CR12 "Lin, G.-M., Warden-Rothman, R. & Voigt, C. A. Retrosynthetic design of metabolic pathways to chemicals not found in nature. Curr. Opin. Sys. Biol. 14, 82 (2019)."). Biology is able to build complex-looking structures with repetitious reactions performed on highly functionalized molecules by enzymes with precise specificity and regioselectivity. The illusion of complexity comes from the difficult and circumvent chemical routes required to recreate the molecule. New chemical spaces will be accessible when the power of both chemistry and biology are seamlessly fused, rather than looking to either alone. Enzymes can be incorporated into retrosynthetic routes by mining them from databases using DNA synthesis and screening and controlling their specificity using evolutionary and computational methods[25](#ref-CR25 "Mazurenko, S., Prokop, Z. & Damborsky, J. Machine learning in enzyme engineering. ACS Catal. 10, 1210 (2020)."),[26](#ref-CR26 "Chowdhury, R. & Maranas, C. D. From directed evolution to computational enzyme engineering - A review. AiChE J. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1002/aic.16847.
                  
                 (2019)."),[27](#ref-CR27 "Bayer, T. S. et al. Synthesis of methyl halides from biomass using engineered microbes. J. Am. Chem. Soc. 131, 6508 (2009)."),[28](/articles/s41467-020-20122-2#ref-CR28 "Dunham, N. P. & Arnold, F. H. Nature’s machinery, repurposed: expanding the repertoire of iron-dependent oxygenases. ACS Catal. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1021/acscatal.0c03606
                  
                 (2020)."). Balancing the constraints of chemical-biological routes “by hand” will be near-impossible, thus requiring design software similar to that which has recently revolutionized chemical retrosynthesis[29](/articles/s41467-020-20122-2#ref-CR29 "Mikulak-Klucznik, B. et al. Computational planning of the synthesis of complex natural products. Nature 
                  https://doi.org/10.1038/s41586-020-2855-y
                  
                 (2020)."),[30](/articles/s41467-020-20122-2#ref-CR30 "Coley, C. W. et al. A robotic platform for flow synthesis of organic compounds informed by AI planning. Science 365, 557 (2019)."). Efficiently manufacturing these compounds will require innovations in determining when steps should be performed in a living chasses versus a cell-free system and innovative modular reactor designs[12](/articles/s41467-020-20122-2#ref-CR12 "Lin, G.-M., Warden-Rothman, R. & Voigt, C. A. Retrosynthetic design of metabolic pathways to chemicals not found in nature. Curr. Opin. Sys. Biol. 14, 82 (2019)."),[30](/articles/s41467-020-20122-2#ref-CR30 "Coley, C. W. et al. A robotic platform for flow synthesis of organic compounds informed by AI planning. Science 365, 557 (2019)."),[31](/articles/s41467-020-20122-2#ref-CR31 "Herschewe, J., Kightlinger, W. & Jewett, M. C. Cell-free systems for accelrating glycoprotein expression and biomanufacturing. J. Ind. Microbiol. Biotechnol. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1007/s10295-020-02321-4
                  
                 (2020)."). The formal marriage of chemistry and biochemistry will radically impact everything from medicines to consumer goods and agrochemicals.

### Hyaline, a thin film for electronics by Zymergen

Zymergen’s hyaline is a polyimide film made from bio-sourced monomers[32](/articles/s41467-020-20122-2#ref-CR32 "Cumbers, J. Inspired by nature, Zymergen brews high-performance bio-electronics. 
                  https://www.forbes.com/sites/johncumbers/2020/04/12/inspired-by-nature-zymergen-brews-high-performance-bio-electronics/#4f18f29f2f18
                  
                 (2020)."),[33](/articles/s41467-020-20122-2#ref-CR33 "McNamara, J., Harvey, J. D., Graham, M. J. & Scherger, C. Optically Transparent Polyimides (ed USPTO) (Zymergen Inc., Akron Polymer Systems Inc., 2020)."). Polyimides, most famously Kapton, are associated with being thermally/chemically stable with superior mechanical properties, but normally they have a coloration that prohibits applications requiring transparency[33](/articles/s41467-020-20122-2#ref-CR33 "McNamara, J., Harvey, J. D., Graham, M. J. & Scherger, C. Optically Transparent Polyimides (ed USPTO) (Zymergen Inc., Akron Polymer Systems Inc., 2020)."). The hyaline family of films are clear, flexible, and mechanically robust making them suitable for flexible electronics (e.g., foldable smartphones and wearable electronics), examples of which will be appearing in products in early 2021[34](/articles/s41467-020-20122-2#ref-CR34 "Dulin, M. Zymergen Reimagines Electronics with Breakthrough Bio-fabricated Materials (Zymergen, Emeryville, CA, 2020)."). The films are made from diamine monomers produced by engineered organisms that were optimized using a suite of robotics to build millions of strains in parallel, with artificial intelligence learning from the failures to design the next round of strains[33](/articles/s41467-020-20122-2#ref-CR33 "McNamara, J., Harvey, J. D., Graham, M. J. & Scherger, C. Optically Transparent Polyimides (ed USPTO) (Zymergen Inc., Akron Polymer Systems Inc., 2020)."),[35](/articles/s41467-020-20122-2#ref-CR35 "Lane, J. Super clear, super thin, super durable: Zymergen bends it like Beckham, electronics-wise. Biofuels Digest (2020)."),[36](/articles/s41467-020-20122-2#ref-CR36 "Serber, Z. et al. Automated System for HTP Genomic Engineering (ed USPTO) (Zymergen, USA, 2020)."). These foundries are emerging globally and accelerating the pace of synthetic biology projects[37](/articles/s41467-020-20122-2#ref-CR37 "Hillson, N. et al. Building a global alliance of biofoudries. Nat. Commun. 10, 2040 (2019).").

Materials have long been sourced from biological sources, but it remains difficult to genetically reprogram the cells to make a new material by design[38](#ref-CR38 "Keating, K. W. & Young, E. M. Synthetic biology for bio-derived structural materials. Curr. Opin. Chem. Eng. 24, 107 (2019)."),[39](#ref-CR39 "van Zyl, E. M. & Coburn, J. M. Hierarchal structure of bacterial-derived cellulose and its impact on biomedical applications. Curr. Opin. Chem. Eng. 24, 122 (2019)."),[40](#ref-CR40 "Gilbert, C. & Ellis, T. Biological engineered living materials: growing functional materials with genetically programmable properties. ACS Synth. Biol. 8, 1 (2019)."),[41](/articles/s41467-020-20122-2#ref-CR41 "Nguyen, P. Q., Courchesne, N.-M. D., Duraj-Thatte, A., Praveschotinunt, P. & Joshi, N. S. Engineered living materials: prospects and challenges for using biological systems to direct the assembly of smart materials. Adv. Mater. 30, 1704847 (2018)."). The first materials produced by synthetic biology were small carbon-based monomers that are green drop-in replacements for petroleum products, such as ethanol (Lanzatech/Total), propanediol (DuPont) or butanediol (Genomatica)[3](/articles/s41467-020-20122-2#ref-CR3 "Renewable Chemical Platforms Building the Biobased Economy. BIO Chemical Companies Report (Biotechnology Innovation Organization, Washington, D.C., 2018)."),[42](#ref-CR42 "Yim, H. et al. Metabolic engineering of Escherichia coli for direct production of 1,4-butanediol. Nat. Chem. Biol. 7, 445 (2011)."),[43](#ref-CR43 "Nakamura, C. E. & Whited, G. M. Metabolic engienering for the microbial production of 1,3-propanediol. Curr. Opin. Biotech. 14, 454 (2003)."),[44](/articles/s41467-020-20122-2#ref-CR44 "Burton, F. Lanzatech, Total and L’oreal Announce A Worldwide Premiere: the Production of the First Cosmetic Plastic Bottle Made from Industrial Carbon Emisssions (Total, 2020)."). Protein-based materials offer the ability to genetically program the order of monomers in a polymer[45](/articles/s41467-020-20122-2#ref-CR45 "Qian, Z.-G., Pan, F. & Xia, X.-X. Synthetic biology for protein-based materials. Curr. Opin. Biotech. 65, 197 (2020)."). Spider silk and related proteins have been produced in fermentations using recombinant cells and prototypes have been announced, such as the Moon Parka by Spiber/North Face[46](/articles/s41467-020-20122-2#ref-CR46 "Cumbers, J. New This Ski Season: A Jacket Brewed Like Spider’s Silk. Forbes (2019)."), but manufacturing challenges appear to have slowed large-scale product releases. They also simplify the design of new materials, such as elastin-silk chimeras that combine their thermal responsiveness and tensile strength[45](/articles/s41467-020-20122-2#ref-CR45 "Qian, Z.-G., Pan, F. & Xia, X.-X. Synthetic biology for protein-based materials. Curr. Opin. Biotech. 65, 197 (2020)."),[47](/articles/s41467-020-20122-2#ref-CR47 "Roberts, E. G. et al. Fabrication and charaterization of recombinant silk-elastin-like-protein (SELP) fiber. Macromol. Biosci. 18, e1800265 (2018)."). Attempts to port this capability to other polymers, such as polyesters, are being made by engineering the ribosome[48](/articles/s41467-020-20122-2#ref-CR48 "Ohta, A., Murakami, H., Higashimura, E. & Suga, H. Synthesis of polyester by means of genetic code reprogramming. Chem. Biol. 14, 1315 (2007)."). Further, biology can control a polymer’s optical or electronic properties by folding it into a nanostructure; for example, the shape of melanin dictates whether it is an UV protectant, luminescent pigment (birds-of-paradise) or photovoltaic cell (wasps)[49](/articles/s41467-020-20122-2#ref-CR49 "Stavenga, D. G., Leertouwer, H. L., Marshall, N. J. & Osorio, D. Dramatic colour changes in a bird of paradise caused by uniquely structured breast feather barbules. Proc. Roy. Soc. B 278, 2098 (2011)."),[50](/articles/s41467-020-20122-2#ref-CR50 "Plotkin, M. et al. Solar energy harvesting in the epicuticle of the oriental hornet (Vespa orientalis). Naturwissenschaften 97, 1067 (2010)."). New computational tools are available to build nanostructures of a defined shape out of DNA or proteins[48](/articles/s41467-020-20122-2#ref-CR48 "Ohta, A., Murakami, H., Higashimura, E. & Suga, H. Synthesis of polyester by means of genetic code reprogramming. Chem. Biol. 14, 1315 (2007)."),[51](/articles/s41467-020-20122-2#ref-CR51 "Seeman, N. C. & Sleiman, H. F. DNA nanotechnology. Nat. Rev. Mater. 3, 17068 (2017)."),[52](/articles/s41467-020-20122-2#ref-CR52 "King, N. P. et al. Accurate design of co-assembling multi-component protein nanomaterials. Nature 510, 103 (2014)."). Biology is not limited to carbon and can build inorganic materials from at least 55 elements, including rare earth and uranium[53](/articles/s41467-020-20122-2#ref-CR53 "Choi, Y. & Lee, S. Y. Biosynthesis of inorganic nanomaterials using microbial cells and bacteriophages. Nat. Rev. Chem. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1038/s41570-020-00221-w
                  
                 (2020)."). Using redox enzymes and engineered phage, inorganic nanomaterials have been made for ultralight batteries, catalysts, solar cells, and optics[53](/articles/s41467-020-20122-2#ref-CR53 "Choi, Y. & Lee, S. Y. Biosynthesis of inorganic nanomaterials using microbial cells and bacteriophages. Nat. Rev. Chem. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1038/s41570-020-00221-w
                  
                 (2020)."),[54](/articles/s41467-020-20122-2#ref-CR54 "Cui, Y. Engineered phage for electronics. Biosens. Bioelectron. 85, 964 (2016).").

## Cells as products

### PROVEN, a biological nitrogen fertilizer for corn by Pivot Bio

Farmers must add nitrogen to crops to obtain high yields, most of which is produced using an industrial chemical process that consumes 1–2% of global energy[55](/articles/s41467-020-20122-2#ref-CR55 "Smil, V. Encriching the Earth: Fritz Haber, Carl Bosch, and the Transformation of the World Food Production. (MIT, Cambridge, MA, 2004)."). Bacteria that fix nitrogen from air are used as biological nitrogen fertilizers, but they are not compatible with cereal crops (corn, wheat, rice). Pivot Bio has created the first biological fertilizer for corn based on a γ-proteobacterium (KV137) that associates with corn roots and has the necessary genes to fix nitrogen. However, the genes are off when most needed, so synthetic biology was used to turn the genes on, which guided the remodeling of the KV137 genome[56](/articles/s41467-020-20122-2#ref-CR56 "Temme, K., Tamsir, A., Bloch, S., Clark, R. & Tung, E. Methods and Compositions for Improving Plant Traits (ed USPTO) (Pivot Bio, Inc., USA, 2020)."). This bacterium is the active ingredient of the liquid fertilizer PROVEN that reduces the need for chemical fertilizer by 25 lbs/acre while increasing yields by 5.8 bushels[57](/articles/s41467-020-20122-2#ref-CR57 "Temme, K. Pivot ProveN Performance Report. (Pivot Bio, 2019)."). Unlike chemical fertilizer, rain does not leach the nitrogen into groundwater, a major source of pollution, or get released into the atmosphere as the powerful greenhouse gas N2O. In 2020, PROVEN was used on 250,000 acres, to be expanded to millions in 2021.

Soil, water, and animals harbor complex microbial communities into which there is the potential to add beneficial functions, or remove harmful ones, from these ecosystems[58](#ref-CR58 "Sheth, R. U., Cabral, V., Chen, S. P. & Wang, H. H. Manipulating bacterial communities by in situ microbiome engineering. Trends Genet. 32, 189 (2016)."),[59](#ref-CR59 "Foo, J. L., Ling, H., Lee, Y. S. & Chang, M. W. Microbiome engineering: current applications and its future. Biotechnol. J. 12, 1600099 (2017)."),[60](/articles/s41467-020-20122-2#ref-CR60 "Nayfach, S. et al. A genomic catalogue of Earth’s microbiomes. Nat. Biotechnol. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1038/s41587-020-0718-6
                  
                 (2020)."). Doing this by introducing a new strain can be challenging as the microbiome environment is poorly characterized, dynamic and ecologically rich[61](/articles/s41467-020-20122-2#ref-CR61 "Ahkami, A. H., White, R. A., Handakumbura, P. P. & Jansson, C. Rhizosphere engineering: enhancing sustainable plant ecosystem productivity. Rhizosphere 3, 233 (2017)."). The best chasses to deliver a new function are from the target environment, as their ability to prosper in this context is non-trivially encoded in their genome; for example, *Pseudomonas simiae* requires 115 genes to optimally colonize a root[62](/articles/s41467-020-20122-2#ref-CR62 "Ke, J., Wang, B. & Yoshikuni, Y. Microbiome engineering: synthetic biology of plant-associated microbiomes in sustainable agriculture. Trends Biotechnol. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.tibtech.2020.07.008
                  
                 (In press) (2020)."). There are many potential chasses from which to choose: microbiomes can be occupied by thousands of species and tools to genetically modify undomesticated bacteria have improved[58](/articles/s41467-020-20122-2#ref-CR58 "Sheth, R. U., Cabral, V., Chen, S. P. & Wang, H. H. Manipulating bacterial communities by in situ microbiome engineering. Trends Genet. 32, 189 (2016)."),[63](/articles/s41467-020-20122-2#ref-CR63 "Lawsn, C. E. et al. Common principles and best practices for engineering microbes. Nat. Rev. Microbiol. 17, 725 (2019)."). Advances in microfluidics, transformation, and genome editing make it easier to culture species and insert large DNA fragments. Principles from control theory can be applied to create genetic systems that do not require extensive re-tuning in a new chassis (“virtual machines”)[64](/articles/s41467-020-20122-2#ref-CR64 "Liu, C. C., Jewett, M. C., Chin, J. W. & Voigt, C. A. Toward an orthogonal central dogma. Nat. Chem. Biol. 14, 103 (2018)."). Using these techniques, engineered plant-associated bacteria are being tested to increase crop yields, protect against pests, and increase the range of climates and soil conditions tolerated[62](/articles/s41467-020-20122-2#ref-CR62 "Ke, J., Wang, B. & Yoshikuni, Y. Microbiome engineering: synthetic biology of plant-associated microbiomes in sustainable agriculture. Trends Biotechnol. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.tibtech.2020.07.008
                  
                 (In press) (2020)."). More broadly, engineered probiotics can vaccinate chickens, protect honey bees against mites, stop malaria from surviving in mosquitos and as human treatments for infections, inflammation, metabolic disorders, and obesity[65](/articles/s41467-020-20122-2#ref-CR65 "Mueller, U. G. & Sachs, J. L. Engineering microbiomes to improve plant and animal health. Trends Microbiol. 23, 606 (2015)."),[66](/articles/s41467-020-20122-2#ref-CR66 "Mimee, M., Citorik, R. J. & Lu, T. K. Microbiome therapeutics - Advances and challenges. Adv. Drug Deliv. Rev. 105, 44–54 (2016).").

### Kymriah, a treatment for B-cell acute lymphoblastic leukemia by Novartis

The therapeutic use of engineered living cells has been described as the “3rd pillar of medicine,” following the era of biologics[67](/articles/s41467-020-20122-2#ref-CR67 "Fischbach, M. A., Bluestone, J. A. & Lim, W. A. Cell-based therapeutics: the next pillar of medicine. Science 179, 1 (2013)."). Kymriah (Tisagenlecleucel) is the first such therapy to afford FDA approval[68](/articles/s41467-020-20122-2#ref-CR68 "Fischer, A. & Stark, A. FDA Approval Brings First Gene Therapy to the United States. (FDA, 2017)."). CAR-T cells are manufactured by isolating the patient’s T cells, genetically modifying them to express a chimeric antigen receptor (CAR) and reintroducing them into a patient, where they can persist for years, even decades[69](/articles/s41467-020-20122-2#ref-CR69 "Hong, M., Clubb, J. D. & Chen, Y. Y. Engineering CAR-T cells for next-generation cancer therapy. Cancer Cell 38, 473 (2020).")

Synthetic biology is playing an increasingly important role in agriculture by offering innovative solutions to address pressing challenges facing the industry. From enhancing crop yields and quality to developing sustainable agricultural practices, synthetic biology is opening new possibilities for agricultural researchers and companies. Using advanced [genetic engineering](https://synbio-tech.com/gene-synthesis) techniques, researchers can now design and optimize crops with specific desirable traits, such as drought resistance or disease tolerance. Additionally, synthetic biology is enabling the development of more efficient and environmentally friendly agricultural practices, such as precision agriculture and biopesticides. As the world’s population continues to grow, the need for more sustainable and efficient agriculture practices becomes increasingly urgent, and synthetic biology is poised to play a critical role in meeting this challenge.

As a scientist in the field of agriculture, you know that the stages of historical development can be categorized into three major stages: primitive agriculture, traditional agriculture, and modern agriculture. Each stage brought a new era of production tools and technology that helped advance the field. Primitive agriculture was characterized by slash-and-burn plowing and stone tool hoeing, while traditional agriculture was marked by the use of iron plows and oxen plowing. Now, modern agriculture has advanced even further with the emergence of agricultural machinery and a focus on mechanization and intelligence. With the continuous scientific advancements in the field of agriculture, it is evident that a new era of growth and progress is on the horizon. Stay ahead of the curve by partnering with a leading agricultural technology company and taking advantage of the latest scientific research and tools to optimize your agriculture production. At Synbio Technologies, we are committed to providing innovative and sustainable solutions to meet your unique needs.

Synthetic Biotechnology Developments

Synthetic biology has emerged as a game-changing technology in the 21st century and is expected to have a profound impact on the future of our world. It has been identified as the third revolution in bioscience, following the groundbreaking discoveries of the double helix structure of DNA and genome sequencing. With the ability to design and engineer biological systems to perform specific functions, synthetic biology has already shown immense potential in fields such as medicine, energy, and agriculture. It has revolutionized the way scientists approach research and development, opening up a world of possibilities for creating new and innovative solutions to some of the most pressing challenges we face, today. As the field continues to evolve and mature, the possibilities for using synthetic biology to transform the world, as we know it, are endless.

![](/wladmin/kindeditor/attached/image/20240726/20240726100544_83199.png)

![](/wladmin/kindeditor/attached/image/20240726/20240726100544_83199.png)

Synthetic biotechnology is a progressive field that applies engineering principles to manipulate organisms and create artificial organisms or products with specific, customized functions. In 2010, the world witnessed the first synthesis of artificial life with the creation of “Synthia 1.0”. Commercial production of a semi-synthetic artemisinin with antimalarial properties followed in 2013. The year 2017 saw a significant breakthrough in synthetic biology, with the successful design and synthesis of five chromosomes of Saccharomyces cerevisiae. In 2021, the world witnessed the introduction of the first-ever artificial cell with active transport and the first self-replicating, living robot. These incredible advancements in synthetic biotechnology are ushering in a new era in modern life science and technology, as we move from simply understanding life to designing and creating it. These developments have the potential to create profound changes in human production methods, lifestyles, and even economic and social structures.

![](/wladmin/kindeditor/attached/image/20240726/20240726100550_13886.png)

![](/wladmin/kindeditor/attached/image/20240726/20240726100550_13886.png)

Gene Synthesis

As an agricultural researcher, you are constantly searching for new and innovative ways to enhance your research, and gene synthesis technology offers just that. With gene synthesis, you can acquire DNA sequences without the need for a template, providing limitless possibilities for synthetic biology research. Synbio Technologies is a leading company in this field, providing precise design and advanced manufacturing that streamlines the entire upstream and downstream industrial chain of DNA synthesis. By utilizing algorithms, AI technology, and bio-intelligent analysis tools, combined with the ability to synthesize hundreds of millions of bases per year, Synbio Technologies drives gene synthesis towards higher intelligence, precision, and speed. With our professional services, we have supported the development of various fields, including molecular breeding, genetic engineering, vaccine development, and DNA information storage, making us an ideal partner for agricultural researchers looking to advance their work. Trust Synbio Technologies to take your research to the next level with our cutting-edge gene synthesis technology.

Service Advantages

Small Genome Synthesis

Researchers are always seeking cutting-edge technologies to advance their work, and gene assembly technology is a promising new development in synthetic biology. With gene assembly technology, you can uncover hidden or novel bioactive synthetic gene clusters and enhance the expression of silenced biosynthetic gene clusters in microorganisms, potentially leading to the synthesis of valuable bioactive substances. With multiple assembly technologies available, such as BioBricksTM, Golden Gate, Gibson, and TAR yeast homologous recombination technology, the possibilities for innovation in your research are limitless.

Synbio Technologies is a leader in gene assembly technology, providing difficult sequence DNA synthesis and assembly solutions that can take your research to the next level. We have developed cutting-edge tools such as CI (Complexity Index), AI-TAT, and our NG Codon system, which enable us to rapidly and accurately synthesize and assemble large DNA fragments. Combined, these tools allow us to deliver 100% correct target sequences while maintaining cost advantages. With Synbio Technologies, you can leverage the power of gene assembly technology to unlock the full potential of your research, staying ahead of the curve in the field of synthetic biology.

Trust Synbio Technologies to be your partner in innovation and discovery. [Contact our](https://synbio-tech.com/contact) team to discuss your next project.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Focus group studies, like those in Austria and Germany, reveal public perceptions and ethical concerns surrounding synthetic biology in agriculture, enhancing understanding of societal impacts.

Utilizing statistical approaches to assess synthetic biology applications in agriculture, such as yield improvements and cost-effectiveness, ensures data-driven decision-making and performance evaluation.

Analyzing different synthetic biology techniques and their outcomes allows for benchmarking against traditional methods, highlighting innovations and effectiveness in crop improvement and sustainability.
